Alright, we're on to step number nine now. This is going quite well, don't you think? This is develop your story and your voice. This is one of the more important steps and it's one of the levels when you raise up, when you move up from where you were before, but you do this by increasing your voice and your story. By voice I mean how you sound, how you present yourself, how your story actually gets told. This is called the million dollar step, the key to becoming persuasive, influential, famous, and notorious and rich. So let's start, we're going to go step by step through this. So what is your credibility? Why should anyone listen to you rather than the other guy? I know we just went through that on the exercise, but this is important. This is something to keep asking yourself, to keep thinking about as you're going along. What is it that's different or what is it that's unique or what is it that's fascinating or what is your story and how is it different from what the other guys' stories are? What are your stories, your life, your career, your reasons for being here selling to your prospect? What brought you here? What was the tale of woe or the long meandering journey or the rollicking adventure that actually brought you here? A lot of people when I talk to them about what is your personal story when I'm interviewing them, when I'm trying to find a hook, when I'm looking for information on stuff, I like to find these background things. And I find that most people can't tell a good story. They say, I grew up in the Midwest and there's not much to tell. That's almost never the case. You have something to tell. Everyone has a story. It's just most of us don't know how to tell it. So I want you to think in terms of three simple acts. The setup, the action, and the resolution. This is actually how a lot of jokes work, how a lot of stand-up comedians do it. The setup is, here's the situation. I was, for myself, for example, my situation when I discovered that it was time for me to start changing my life and become a freelance copywriter. The setup is, I was sleeping on a couch yet again having lost my girlfriend, my job, and my place to live all within a very short period of time. I was going nowhere and I was on someone else's couch after essentially living out of my car. And that's where I was. So that's kind of the setup. Not, you know, it was a bad situation. Then the action was, and I had an epiphany. I realized that there was going to be no white knight to come along and save me. If I was going to change anything, it was going to be up for me. So I got busy thinking about what I could do to actually change things. Where I started reading books, I started going after mentors and I started thinking about how I could actually change things. And the resolution was, that brought me to the point where I started my career. My career got going in earnest and I became a top copywriter and then a top coach for people who wanted to do the same thing. Now, best if you can get it down to three sentences and you can expand later. So for me, it was like, I was one of the worst slackers possible. And then I had a realization that if anything was going to change in my life, it was up to me to be able to change it. So I got busy changing it. And everything morphed into, in my life, into something wonderful. So that's my basic thing. So think about yours in three simple acts. Your story is sometimes a myth, like the, we will talk about ideas like rags to riches, things like that. Or it's a shared iconic situation. Like, for me, it was a revelation or a discovery. Sometimes it's been there, done that, or a raw experience. Or sometimes it's things like hometown boy makes good. That was kind of my story, but not really. Mine was more of a rags to riches. There's loser learns to win. A lot of the headlines I've had use those stories. You know, the guy who nobody thought was going to win the underdog actually got it together and astonished everyone and won against all odds. Clueless dude gets a clue. That was certainly my story. I was just the most clueless guy you ever met. And my whole journey was about getting clues, following up on them, and then making them manifest in my life. Outsider sneaks inside. That's a big one. You can say I was just like you. I was on the outside looking in. However, I got inside. I started learning how the people who know how to do these things do them. And how they live better lives. And I'm going to share that with you. Also, the regular guy discovers the secrets of the exceptionality, which is say you played baseball and you were an okay person playing baseball until you discovered a mentor who taught you the secrets of moving up a level. So you weren't just a good baseball player. You became an exceptional baseball player. So these kind of stories all resonate with people. And if you think about the literature and the history of movies and stories in our culture, they all fit into these basic storylines. Storytelling is the oldest form of transferring information. Our brains are actually hardwired to explain things and receive information via stories. Before there was writing, before there were newspapers, before there was television and radio. The only way we had to convey information was through stories. So you told people stories about how our God came down and gave us fire. And that became a story about teaching young people how to respect fire and that if it ever went away, there was a way to get it back, but you had to learn how to do it. So and also the myth of various villages and tribes. All of these were carried off from generation to generation through stories because until we got to the place where we could write books and reports and get it written down, the only record was by telling stories. So our brains are love stories. We love to hear them. We love to tell them. We just kind of lost the ability to do it very well. Yeah, the reasons we like it is the structure that good stories take. It fires up your interests. You make a big promise. There is some drama or some humor or some mystery and then it all pays off with a delivery of the big promise. So like an Agatha Christie mystery novel is, you know, a bunch of people got together in an old spooky house and then they start dying off one by one. So that's that's kind of building up the stuff. And then there's there's a payoff in that the big promise is that you think the detective is going to solve it at the end. But how he's going to do it and what the solution is going to be. You don't know. So you stick with it and you're involved. These are great stories. So start with your own family and your own life up to now. What happened to bring you where you are today? All families have stories. Some of the best I grew up in a family full of storytellers. And in fact, because I was 10 years younger than my brother, seven years younger than my sister, bit of an afterthought. I sat at a table where I watched my parents and my siblings tell riveting stories about what happened to them during the day and what was going on and gossip and rumors and all that. And I was a little kid and I learned early on that if I didn't have a point to my story, I wasn't going to get listened to. So if I just said, well, I was in the sandbox today and I wouldn't have an audience. So I started having to concoct stories and get things going. I was kind of lucky in that. But even people who don't tell stories normally in their families, the family still has stories. If you get going with this and you start becoming a very good writer and a prolific writer, one of the things you should probably do is write down your family history for your children, for your children's children, for the rest of the family that didn't pay attention to what was going on, where your family came from. But it's not a boring recital of Joe was born in Ohio and then he moved to California and met mom and that's how the whole family came out. No, it was like, what was he doing in Ohio? What was his family like? What was the situation that what drove him to move to move from there to here? So you have stories. You have the story about how you graduated high school and the embarrassing thing that happened to you when you got up on stage to collect your diploma. We all have these stories. We tend to forget them as a salesman and as a as a business owner who is trying to make some money. And as a writer who is taking on clients, you need to understand stories. You need to develop your own and you need to become you need to become one with the idea of being a storyteller. So study great storytellers. Ted talks are great. Not all of them are great storytellers, but they all get up and within the paradigms of the TED talks, which is a very short period of time. One subject they have they have rules about how they deliver it. How do they do it? Break it down. Think critically. What made it interesting for the stuff that was interesting to you? At what point was your interest triggered? At what point did it did it raise to the point that I can't wait to find out what this guy is talking about? What kept you interested? What didn't keep you interested? What turned you off? What made you click and get off of that to talk and go to another one? Great social media items that go viral. Yeah, there's a lot of really bad cat videos out there and things like that. But there's also a lot of great stuff that goes viral, especially podcasts and things that are good because they tell good stories. And there's always some kind of a story. Even the dumb cat videos. There's a story. Where was this cat? Why was it jumping up and down like that? Why was the coyote playing with that with that baseball out in the backyard? Who's doing it? Where are they? There's all kinds of things involved in this. So this is all about getting interest going with the tales. Do not bore your listener. That is the number one rule. You may not be telling the most adventurous, rollicking tale that is possible in the history of mankind. But if you're delivering information, the one thing you cannot do is bore them. You can even take an interesting story and go off on so many tangents or stumble around or not make a point to actually do a bad job telling what should have been a good story in a bad way. So trim details to the essentials. Shoot for that three sentence outline that leaves them wanting more. The setup, the action and the resolution. You get good through telling and retelling stories. That is, this is one of those things where there's no pill you can take and I can't magically transform the ability to tell good stories to you. You have to just start. Start with short stories. Start telling them. Start critically examining other people that you know. Articles and books that you read. Things that you see on television or at the movies or videos you're watching online or whatever. What made it a good story? When did your interest get triggered? So start thinking about how you can use those tactics. The National Enquirer, I'm not sure if it's even on the stands anymore, but all of the writers I know had a period where we fell in love with the National Enquirer, which was the magazine that is now being copied online. And I think they do exist online, but they just had outrageous headlines. And they, before there was clickbait, they were pop culture clickbait. You bought the National Enquirer because you were standing in a line at the market and it was right there in the headline. It was so riveting that you had to get it and then you turned to check out and you couldn't put it back and you wanted to find out more. So you bought it. They sold, they outsold everybody else in the country at the height of their popularity. Nobody admitted to reading the National Enquirer because it was, again, it was clickbait. It was outrageous stuff, but they sold so much that it was pretty obvious that a lot of people were buying it that weren't admitting to buying it. So it was all about the hook, bringing people in. What, you know, so ask yourself, look at some examples of any kind of clickbait out there, especially look at the National Enquirer old ones, if you can. How did they hook you? How were the details shared? When was the big promise made? You know, if you read this, you will learn blah or you will know about blah. And when was it paid off? At what point in the story? So here's an example I like to do. What happened to you today? It's never nothing, though a lot of people will answer, nothing happened to me today. Just living provides good fodder for stories. In seminars, I used to tell people when they took a lunch break to A, don't go out with a friend, go out with a stranger, but also to come back with a story. And it's like just getting in the elevator in a hotel and going up. It's never, I got in the elevator, nothing happened, and then I went to my room. That isn't it. It's like, it's like, yeah, I was in there and there were two kids shivering, who had just gotten out of the pool and they had lost their room key at the bottom of the pool. And they were going to go in and hope that mom and dad were there because they otherwise were locked out of the room. Or there were a couple of guys from a Shriners convention there who obviously drunk and they weren't handling it very well. And they were telling bad jokes. Or it looked like there was a couple in there that were having an argument. All of this stuff is happening. So expand your awareness. Think about the world as your, as your storytelling provider, all of the details that happen to you. I think it's very important to know that nothing doesn't happen. Something happens to you. You may, for the sake of not living a life where you remember every single detail of the trip you took down to the store to get milk. But if something does happen, you should be able to tell the story so that it makes sense. It transfers the excitement you had or the how scared you were or how different it was this time that you went. Don't think in terms of novels, but rather in short stories or jokes. It's your turn around the campfire on a subject. I know people don't sit around campfires much more and doing this, but people used to, I was in Boy Scouts and you had one or two stories that when it was your turn to tell a story, you had a story. It might be a story you learned from someone else about a snipe hut or something. Or it was something that happened to you on a hike. You know, you came up on a rattlesnake or, you know, Joe fell over and rolled halfway down the hill before you could catch up with them or something like that. But what is the story? Think about the terms in short stories. Jokes are very good because jokes are very short. They're very condensed. And they usually follow the setup action in Resolution 3 Act play thing. Write in the language of your audience when you're writing stories or when you're telling stories. You use the slang that your audience understands. You make the same assumptions that they make. You have the same biases and the same knowledge base that they have. Don't introduce new language or new slang or don't make assumptions that aren't true with your audience. That's very, very good. You with good story tell you want to slide right into the conversation that's already going on in the head of your subject. My recommendation to everybody has become a ravenous reader. I know reading is out of favor now with a lot of people, but all of the top writers and all of the top marketers I know read a lot. They read, yeah, business. They also read self help, but they read a lot of fiction. And the more popular fiction, the better for a lot of copywriters. I order them to read a Daniel Steele novel. I think there's one in the bookcase behind me. It's because she's one of the bestselling authors of all time. It's not because it's good. It's because if this is what people are reading, read it critically and think, what is it that these people are doing that I didn't notice? Or what, you know, how is it going right? I read a lot of hacks and I read a lot of masters at all. A lot of nonfiction and a lot of fiction masters and also the hacks. If they're bestsellers, because I want to know what it is that they're doing, how they're distilling the story. So it's it's being read by mass numbers of people. The heartbeat of great copy is the emotional impact. It's the difference between dead copy and copy that actually lives. It involves empathy and a rollicking tale. You got to get into the head of your listener or your prospect or your viewer and understand where they're coming from and give them a rollicking tale. Usually, if you have a great story to tell, that passion will show through, but you have to keep the listener or the watcher involved. Your job is to, again, wake up the scenario blob in the zombie zone. People are out there, they're snoring, they're snoozing, they're in a white white noise state. There's a lot of fuzz going on. They're just kind of clicking through channels or they're browsing through magazines or they're clicking and surfing the web. So you want to wake them up and you want to paint a pleasing picture in your prospect's mind or a rousing picture in their mind. So you want to start that visualization process. You want to start telling them the story that's going to affect them, that's going to increase their enjoyment of the day or make them a little uncomfortable because they realize that, wow, I need to get out of my comfort zone. Or you're going to shock them or you're going to fascinate them. But you do want to be the one thing that he sees today that gets his blood moving because you want to get him out of that passive state into an active state. So strive for significant points where your help, your product, your information will thrill them. You want to be able to say seriously and without second thought, remember this day, from here forward, everything will change for you. You may not put that in your copy, you may put that in your copy, I've certainly used that. But the spirit of that should be there all the time when you're telling stories. Everything changes after this point. What a lucky boy you are that you stumbled on this. I want you to read you the movie, which is a blog post I had. We'll include that with the materials. But I want you to think in term of your scripts. You, the movie, was a piece I wrote which actually has been picked up by other online publications and used a lot. It's about thinking of your life in terms of a movie. You're actually in charge of the script. You can have a lot more input into how your movie, which is your life, turns out than anybody previously thought. A lot of people think you're just kind of buffeted about by the universe and whatever happens is going to push you into whatever happens. But you have a lot of say in how you react to the things that happen to you. You have a lot of choices to make. So when you start thinking about yourself as a movie and you're writing the script, you have more control over it. It also helps you think in storytelling terms. I want everyone to keep a journal. If you haven't before, start now. If you have before, do it in the right way. Don't do a simple diary thing like woke up, had lunch, went to work, came home, went to bed. Don't do that. And don't bitch and moan about stuff. Don't go on and on about the wrongs that happen to you. But rather, write to yourself years from now knowing that you'll pick it up, not to anybody else, just for yourself. Nobody else needs to read this. But write to yourself in the future and explain what it was like to be alive in this time right now for you. So that your future self will look back and be able to really feel what it was like in these days. How you're feeling, what happened, how things are going, how you think things are going to turn out, how you hope things are going to turn out, how things turned out from yesterday that happened. You don't need to do a lot, but I think sitting down and writing from 20 minutes to an hour every day really helps keep you centered. And it's, it, it, writers write. So if you're going to start writing for yourself to be able to sell, any kind of writing that you do is going to help. It helps you start thinking in the terms of storytelling. It helps you develop your voice as you're talking or as you're, as you're writing or, you know, whether it's a script that you're going to, you're going to speak or whether it's, whether it's going to be on a written page. Journals are something that all writers and marketers of the upper echelons, they keep them. Sometimes they destroy them, sometimes they leave them for posterity, sometimes they write them to be read later. But always, this is a tool for you. And it's one of the things that writers come back to me years later and say, I'm glad you ordered me to write a journal. I've, you know, I've kept it for years and I've never stopped writing. It's a good habit and it's helped me in all ways. I'm a better writer. I'm a better storyteller. I'm able to more critically think about what happened to me during the day and what's going to happen tomorrow. My stories, for example, are a couple of easy ones. I've told them so many times that they fall into kind of a quick tell. So my fifth midlife crisis was I decided to, I was in my forties. By the way, I've had about seven midlife crises and I've enjoyed every single one of them. They were the best things that ever happened to me. And the fifth one, though, was particularly interesting. I just decided to quit business and told my clients, I'm out of here. I don't know how long I'm going to be gone. It turned out to be a little over a year. And I formed a rock and roll band. I found a drummer living in a dry camp outside of Virginia City, Nevada. I had to leave notes for him around town because he had no phone and no electricity. But he's one of the best drummers I'd ever come across in my life. And he was game for starting a band. And I found a bass player who was 20 years younger than me, but who had the same spirit that I did and actually liked the same rock and roll songs. So we got together, the three of us, and we put together a set list. And it was the kind of set list that met two criteria. The song made it into the set list either had to make you want to cry in your beer or shake your booty. And we only allowed one or two cry in your beer songs per set. And the rest of the time we would do sometimes an hour straight of nothing but hard driving rock and roll that had people dancing and sweating. They had the best time of their life, but they went home happy, but wet and damp. And it was a great time. And we toured all the biker bars and dives in northern Nevada. And it was just a great time. And I have a lot of other stories about that. The basic thing, the midlife crisis. I quit the rat race. I went off and had a three-piece rock band that we toured the biker bars in northern Nevada. Mom's Kitchen Radio is a thing also about music, which was one of my... When I was growing up I was around 12 or 13. And my mother had always had a table radio and she had it tuned to country music. So I grew up with mostly country music, Johnny Cash, Patsy Klein, Kitty Wells, Bob Wills and the Texas Playboys, that kind of stuff. Good stuff. But occasionally she'd go over to rock. But one day when my hormones were starting to kick in, she had inexplicably changed the station from K-WOW down in Southern California, which was a country station, to KRLA, which was one of the great rock and roll stations down there in the early 60s. And the song that came on was The Kinks, You Really Got Me. And as a... I think I might have just turned 13 or maybe it was late 12. The song just stopped me cold and I swooned. I actually almost sank to my knees. And I was overwhelmed with how great this song was. And it was my introduction to my kind of music. It's what got me into rock and roll. It's what... It fired up a passion that had previously not been there. And I went from... That changed my life. I went from being a young adolescent who was happy with playing army and baseball and being a Boy Scouts to suddenly needing a guitar. Not wanting a guitar, needing a guitar. And of course my story about my couch epiphany I've told a lot. I lost my job, my girlfriend and my place to live all within a few months period. I was living out of my car. I was broke, going nowhere. I was 30 years old. And I had an epiphany on the couch that if anything was going to change, I was going to be the agent in change and I had to get busy. And from there the story goes deeper. But these are the titles of my stories and I have hundreds of these. And all of my good friends are storytellers too and they have hundreds. And they can pull out those stories at a time. My business partners Stan and I, Stan's one of the best storytellers that I've hung out with. And we've gone off on adventures over our 30 year friendship and business career. And we both kind of watch each other as things happen because we've had a lot of adventures happen to us too. And we're kind of forming the story as we're going along. We're living it. And we're kind of trading things. And at the end of the day we will compare our stories. And some of us have a, one time he'll have a better take. Sometimes I'll have a better take or a funnier take. But it's funny we know critically how these stories develop because we've been doing it for a long time. Experience helps. That doesn't stop you from starting now though, even if you haven't told stories before. Words are your tools. Learn to tell stories with expressions and movement in person. Then translate that into written copy. When you're telling a story to somebody live it's really easy to point, to make expressions, to kind of illustrate what's going on. The trick is to learn how to translate that into written copy or into audio if you're using video sales letters. Or to use them correctly in video if you are videotaping yourself. Words are your tools and the expressions and movements either translate through words or they help the words do their job. You are your prospects eyes, ears, nose and emotional proxy. You can't assume anything. You have to be the one that says, I was scared. I was so scared that blog. I turned the corner and the scene that unfolded before me was shocking. What I saw was this. What I smelled was this. What I heard was this. You have to be the person that completes everything there. Finally, I'll say this again. Not fine, I think there's a few more. But writers write. Your voice will come from writing critically. So write and pay attention to what you're writing and look at it and think, is that the best way I could tell that? Is that the right words I should be using? Is this the best way I can present this particular part of what I'm trying to say? You're going to grow with each story. Keep making them better. It's okay to be bad at this at first. But this is how your voice develops. And by voice, of course, we're talking about the way you tell something. You may start out being boring. You may stutter. You may stammer. You may not be able to make your point. Get better. Do it better next time. Think about how to do it. Critically examine what you said and how you could say that better. You're going to find your voice. Don't copy anyone else. Your voice is going to matter. Don't try to impress another writer, by the way. Just learn to tell your story using your temple, your style, and your experience. This is how your voice starts to evolve. So you don't sound like someone else. You're going to sound like you. And this goes back to let verbs do the work. So you have the power word report, which is a good place to look. A lot of writers use that power word report. They have it on their desk all the time. It's doggier. They use it for inspiration and for double checking of their own vocabulary. Every word must earn its place. Strip out unnecessary words ruthlessly. For a headline, for example, you know how you got the headline right? Is when you can't strip out another word without changing the meaning of the sentence. That's how you find out if the headline is as terse and solid as possible. There's enough words in there to tell the story, to get across the point you want to make. And nothing extraneous. I do this with almost every sentence I have and every piece of copy I write. I want to strip out unnecessary words. I want to strip things down so they're powerful. And there's no extraneous verbiage in there. That's why verbs do a lot of work. Adjectives, one of my piece of advice to rookie writers, don't use adjectives at all. Let the verbs do the work. If you don't know the difference between a verb and an adjective, you have a piece of homework now to go do. Go find out what the difference is. And a book that I recommend is The Elements of Style by Strunk and White. It's a very short book. You can read it in about an hour and a half. But it's for anyone who's going to write, it solves all those questions you have that you never learned in school about how to correctly put a sentence together. It's actually an exciting little book. It's very well written. I think White, E.B. White was the guy who wrote Charlotte's Web. But this is a basic piece that you should really pay attention to. And to understand flow, copy some great ads by hand. Not by memory, but you want to feel what it's like to write like that. Top writers have done this. Some of the best writers you know of. Some of the best fiction writers have done this. Take a piece of writing from a writer you respect and write it down by hand, exactly as they've written it. Now, the reason we say not memory is that sometimes people will say, yeah, I got the gist of it. I don't need to do that. And then I'll ask them, OK, write down what I just said word for word. Or write down what you just read word for word. And they'll get it wrong. They'll change words around. They'll change the punctuation. They'll insert things that weren't there because we're processing stuff all the time. So if you want to understand how great copy feels, write it down exactly as it was written. Hunter Thompson did that with Hemingway, for example. He typed out whole books by Hemingway because he wanted to feel what it was like to write like that. And again, a lot of top writers do this. You don't need to do this very long, but you need to do it to understand the flow. Because if you think you've got it down, if you think, yeah, I understand how you wrote that. And then if you can't recite it or you can't write it down exactly as it was done, then you're missing a lot of the elements of flow, how the thing flows together and moves critically. There's a songwriting element to this. There's a melodic nature of great writing. Da, da, da, da, da, da, da, da. I write in terms of lyrics all the time. I think there's a melody going on in my head as I'm writing. And as you read my writing, if you read it out loud, it flows naturally. It's not just in the nature of good conversation. It's in the nature of melodic conversation. I study great lyric writers, for example, like what's his name from The Rolling Stones? Mick Jagger is one of the best lyric writers in the business. And he writes stuff like I met a gin-soaked bar room queen in Memphis. And that just rolls off the tongue. There are no extraneous words in there. And just trying to read that plane, you can't. That's off of Honky Tonk Women, by the way. Bruce Springsteen is another great writer. In the day we sweat it out on the streets of a runaway American dream. It's got rhythm. It's got melody to it. It's not boring writing. Again, there's no extraneous words in there. Because they have to write to a very tight paradigm where it has to fit in with the melody. You should write like that, too. You should study good writing, good lyrics, and pay attention to how melodically the stuff rolls off of your tongue. Great song lyrics are pithy. They tell a story. They come at you in a flow, like I said, the Stones and Bruce. The right word matters, both for rhythm and moving the story along. Sometimes you may have to sit there during your edit and find the right word. Writers rewrite. Good writers rewrite stuff all the time. I have done multiple rewrites of ads. Some of my best ads were rewritten up to a dozen times and more. And a lot of it was not making major changes, but searching for the right word. Trying different words, trying different ways of saying things. You can take a boring story and turn it into an interesting story. Just with a few word changes often. And a lot of this has to do with the rhythm and the flow of the story. You know when you're done, when you cannot strip out a single word or phrase without changing the meaning of the sentence. That is the key that you want to write for. Eventually you will start having copy and stories that dance inside of your prospect's head. And that's where he will devour it. He will remember it. And you have a better shot at becoming influential. Think about that dance that's going on with the words that you deliver. Strive for memorable quotes, one-liners, slogans, titles and subtitles. This isn't literature, it's selling. So even though I don't like slogans on their own, if you can write a headline or a subhead or a caption or a piece of copy that comes off like a slogan, that is memorable that they think, oh yeah, even when they're done reading they think back on it, then that's very good. When you're telling stories, those one-liners, the slogans, those are the things that matter. Use alliteration and rhyme shamelessly. People unconsciously speak when reading. Make it effortless to go from their brain to their heart, excuse me, through their eye, to their brain, to their heart. Alliteration, if you don't know what that means, you should look it up, but basically it's using similar sounds over and over again. I think you'll see that if you study my copy. And rhyme makes for memorable stuff. That's why slogans that stores and large businesses use often rhyme. It's just easier to remember. So here's some examples. This is for golf, the golf market too. It's so easy you'll be laughing after every explosive drive. This brilliant new breakthrough by scientific golf geeks reveals a breathtakingly simple and super fast way for you to instantly trick your swing to tap the hidden payload of raw power that you've been ignoring your entire life. But hey, don't believe me because you can check it out for free yourself. Hi Joe, well, science has finally come to golf, took them long enough. I mean, you can only waste your time on projects like landing on Mars and carrying baldness for so long. Before you decide you have to pay some attention to something really serious like golf. There's a bounce to that. There's a rhythm to that. Read it over, copy it if you want, understand how that works. And here's a headline beginning a story in a certain voice. How you can profit from the coming stock market crash and financial bloodbath caused by cash rich drug dealers and other criminal scum. A lot of C's in there, the C sound that would be the alliteration, how you can, coming, crash, cause, cash rich, criminal scum. And it just, this is in a particular voice. This was actually a financial report that did quite well. Now, your credibility. Establish truthfully and forcefully but without hype why you're the go-to guy in this situation. Remember your audience, your perfect prospect. He's in some kind of pain. He's in some kind of dilemma. He's skeptical. He's ready to bolt. He will not put up with bragging. You must win him over on his terms immediately. So, we're credibility. We're you being the guy who can stand up and say, I'm the guy that should be telling you this because, blank, where credibility intersects with your story is where you start developing your voice. This is how you start thinking about the conversation you would have face to face and translating that riveting conversation into a copy or a script or audio. The go-to guy is the adult in the room who has never rattled. It's the dude you want with the ball in crunch time. It's the clever veteran of life with good ideas and plans that actually work. People will pay you to be in control for this particular situation. When you have good credibility, you are the go-to guy. And that kind of sums that up. Very important part, don't worry about your voice right now, but it will come to you and remember that writer's right, and so get that journal going. Alright, storytelling, finding your voice. The questions are very individual. So rather than try to pick some of the best questions, because you're probably going to have your own if you're in the coaching program, however, I wanted more talk about what is the most common instructor feedback. So maybe we can save you one, go around working with your coach if you're in the simple writing system coaching program. That has to do with, the old expression, at least the one I heard going up, was preaching to the choir. Or just, you've mentioned as, don't bore your reader. And again, another one of those tips you've told me that remarkably works almost every time is you end up throwing away the first draft. So just talk a little bit about that. As you think of writers, writing is rewriting. Just talk a little bit more about people, how the process really works when you're... One of the old tactics that the copywriters that I grew up emulating and learning from and mentoring under, was there was a tactic that we had called just toss the first couple of pages. And what that meant was that in the first draft, we would just dump everything we could into the first manuscript or the first draft of whatever we were going to write. And when we went back, we gave ourselves permission to say, you know, we're kind of the equivalent of clearing our throat, as we say, we're over explaining, we're doing stuff. And what actually happened more often than not, and still happens with top writers, is the actual copy starts halfway down the first page or at the end of the first page. So you actually toss the first part of what you've done. And this is so common, it's just because you're getting your brain going and in an early draft, you're just writing a lot of extra stuff. So a real mistake that writers make is just think that the first thing they write is a masterpiece. And that's going to be, you know, that they should sit down, write it and be done. And that's just not the case. Writing is rewriting. And sitting down and going over it a few times and deciding this can come out and this can come out is good. And one of the first things you will do, if you find it's clunky or it's just not working, often just take the first page, toss it, start in the middle of the story. Start with the action. Yeah, again, a common thing that we'll see people send and submit their drafts is let's say you've developed some of your hooks if you're in the pain relief market. Just to take an example that people should relate to. Once you've hooked the reader in, whether it's your video or your script, you need to spend almost no time reminding them that pain is bad. Like they know that. They know the problems of this. You may need to establish that you understand that, but sometimes people, it'll go on for pages. It's one of the most... Going over and over and over things that they wouldn't be reading if they didn't experience. So get right to it. It's one of the most common problems they have is they... As a writer, you explain to the reader what they're going through and you don't need to do that. They know that's why they're here reading your piece, because they know that pain sucks. They know that it's hard to get up in the morning and hard to do the things you want to do. A lot of commercials on TV you'll see, especially in that market, make this mistake. They're preaching to the choir. They are explaining how the life is to the prospect. You don't need to do that. They're already on board. They are already having this. The reason this happens is because a lot of writers don't share the problems of the audience they're going after. So they feel real proud that they have all this empathy and they understand what life is like. But that's not what resonates with somebody who's looking for relief. Exactly. The process of learning what their life is like is critical. Background. Yeah, exactly. That becomes background. You don't need to put every word of that into the script. Okay, two specific questions. We've touched on this theme a couple of times before. The examples you gave, the voice and the story is a personal story. Do you do anything different if it's a business you're marketing? It's not Bob the founder anymore. It's XYZ Corp does this thing. It's a service. It's a product. Anything different about you saying that? Yes. When you're writing for a client it's hard to assign a voice to his business, to his corporation. That's why I like to choose someone in the business to be the face of the advertising. That's why in golf I've used Doc O'Leary. He's actually one of the owners. They call him Doc. He was a nickname but people need to know his real name. Doc O'Leary is a great little handle. I actually started mimicking his voice. It's very much like he talks but it's through the way I translate it. Even though it's a business, it's a real corporation, he has a certain way of describing things. It's very folksy. Of course this is in the golf market. It's a very high passion market. In other markets, if you can, you should because a voice helps get the sales message across. A voice helps the story be told. Sometimes you'll get resistance from that. It's like if you have a pet store, for example. A pet store doesn't really have a voice but the owner of the pet store may have a voice. That's why you'll see a lot of really good advertising, especially local advertising, makes a star out of the owner or out of one of the clerks or one of the people involved with this. It's trying to find a voice, trying to find what is the sound of the pet store. It's the owner often. That's where possible. Personalize the thing. People buy from people. The last question I'm going to ask, a common application. Many people who go through the program, they're professional writers where they're going to be right for others. Or even if you're the entrepreneur, this is you're doing this for the sake of your own business, your own sales, even one of the examples you put up was what we call affiliate marketing. You're going to promote other people's products as one of your income streams. In that case, they've got their voice. What are some tips you could give on just a couple, one, two, three points where you're not going to be able to control the entire story because at best you're going to hand off to their story? You know, you know your audience better than anyone. So if you're doing affiliate marketing, you're going to your list, right? So you know what they have. So if you make it from you, then you can have your voice and your story. You can actually introduce things. You can say, I had an example of it. I don't usually pitch other people's stuff, but this was so important to me. I really wanted you to know it. So, and then you can say, let me hand it off to Joe and he will explain what's going on. So your voice gets them into the story, you hand it off to Joe and then whatever the affiliate stuff is can be there. But because you're going to your list as an affiliate marketer, you have control over, you know, the voice up to the point that you have to abandon it and let the other person take over. Right, okay, thanks. All right, that wrapped up this section. John will introduce the exercise to you and then we'll continue on with the simple writing system. All right, here's the exercise I want you to go through. I want you to tell your story in three stages. It can be three sentences. In fact, aim for having three complete sentences be each part of the three acts. Give a little bit about how you got here. Give a little bit about what's going on and a little bit on what kind of end to the story can a prospect expect. Pretty much what you would say to someone who sat down at the bar next to you in that situation we've talked about and explained and said something that identified them as the perfect prospect. So what would you say in a very short thing? Here's who I am. Here's what's going on. Here's what you might expect if you got involved yourself. So give that a try. It's a good start to writing stories.
